crux_variable: Royalty rate per chip (blended) trajectory: v9/CSS migration rate and data center share of royalty pool
thesis_one_line: ARM's royalty rate expansion story breaks on two simultaneous mechanisms -- the Qualcomm ALA legal precedent caps the rate ARM can charge its largest custom-CPU customers, while China RISC-V displacement erodes the 22% of revenue ARM is already earning from a territory its own JV structure cannot fully protect -- and at 143x NTM P/E, a revenue trajectory that is merely 'good but not great' triggers a violent de-rating to IP-licensor comps, not a soft landing.

key_debates.debate: Debate 1 (the crux): The Qualcomm ALA precedent caps ARM's royalty rate on its highest-ASP customers
key_debates.bear_read: The royalty rate expansion thesis depends on migrating ALA licensees (who hold a blanket license to ARM's ISA) to CSS-level contracts at >10% of chip ASP. In October 2025, Qualcomm won the Nuvia case: the Delaware court ruled that Qualcomm's acquisition of Nuvia did not require it to renegotiate its ALA at higher TLA rates. ARM's appeal is pending, but the court of first instance ruling means that any ALA licensee that designs a custom CPU core can argue it owes only the original ALA rate -- not the CSS rate ARM now charges under its own compute subsystem licensing. Qualcomm is ARM's single largest high-ASP royalty payer in the smartphone segment, which is the base of the royalty pool. If Qualcomm's ALA argument prevails (the appeal fails, as the court of first instance already ruled), other large ALA holders -- MediaTek, Samsung, potentially Apple through its ARM architecture history -- can cite the same precedent to resist rate renegotiation. The base case (Daedalus, core IP NOPAT $4.46B by FY2030) requires CSS reaching 15% of royalty revenue by FY2028; if CSS migration is capped at TLA-only licensees, CSS cannot reach even 8% of royalty revenue by FY2028 because the majority of custom-CPU-design volume sits in ALA holders. The v9 migration (from 31% to 55% of royalty revenue) still proceeds for non-custom licensees, but v9 earns only ~5% of chip ASP versus CSS's >10% -- and the incremental lift from the CSS tier is the lever that converts the base into the bull. Without it, blended royalty revenue growth slows from the base's 17.2% 4-year CAGR to approximately 10-12%.
key_debates.break_mechanism: ARM loses the Qualcomm appeal (or the appeal is tied up for 2+ years without prejudgment injunction). Any ALA licensee that subsequently receives a demand for CSS renegotiation cites the Delaware ruling as precedent. The mechanism: ALA licensees hold a 'blanket' license to implement any ARM architecture generation; ARM's argument that CSS constitutes a new product requiring a new contract was rejected by the trial court; on appeal, the burden to overturn factual findings is high. Watching for: ARM quarterly earnings calls disclosing any licensing-dispute reserve or reduced ALA licensee count; any public statements by MediaTek or Samsung disputing rate renegotiation; the Delaware Court of Chancery appellate docket for ARM Holdings v Qualcomm Technologies (filed October 2025).
key_debates.what_it_is_worth: CSS migration from 5% to >10% of chip ASP on the custom-CPU segment adds approximately $700-900M of incremental annual royalty revenue by FY2028 in the base case (Theia: $500M+ annually at a 40x exit multiple worth ~$20B EV / ~$19/share). If CSS is capped at TLA-only licensees, this increment is roughly halved to $300-400M. The shortfall versus base: ~$400-500M of FY2028 annual royalty revenue foregone, or roughly $400M x 17.05x exit multiple / 1.4909 discount factor = ~$4.57B EV reduction, ~$4.29/share below the base SOTP. Compounded through the NOPAT path (lower FY2029-FY2030 NOPAT from both the rate miss and the license-pipeline signal), the total impact in the bear scenario is approximately $7-9/share below Daedalus's $57.36 base.
evidence: Delaware court ruling in ARM Holdings v Qualcomm Technologies (October 2025): trial court ruled Qualcomm's ALA entitles it to custom CPU design without renegotiation to TLA/CSS terms. Source: Tom's Hardware analysis, cited in ARM_crux.json crux_decomposition.driver_3_ceiling.qualcomm_legal_risk_cap.

ARM_crux.json key_debates[0].bear_read: 'The Qualcomm legal precedent could cap the rate ARM can charge ALA licensees for custom designs. If other large ALA holders (Qualcomm, potentially others) invoke the same argument, ARM's rate upside is limited to TLA licensees only.'

ARM_market_facts.json analyst_tape.post_earnings_analyst_actions[0]: Morgan Stanley downgrade April 2026, citing 'potential conflicts with existing IP licensees' as a named risk alongside rising R&D costs.
ARM_valuation.json scenarios[2].reasoning (bear): 'Qualcomm appeal outcome caps royalty rate upside for ALA licensees' custom designs' as the primary mechanism in Daedalus's own bear case.

v9 at 31% of royalty revenue, CSS still at early innings (ARM_crux.json driver_2_rate.v9_share_of_royalty_rev_fy2026_q4): without CSS acceleration, the v9 migration alone is the rate story, and v9 at ~5% of ASP vs CSS at >10% means the incremental kicker does not materialize.
key_debates.historical_precedent: Qualcomm's own licensing dispute with Apple (2017-2019): Apple disputed Qualcomm's royalty rates and withheld payments across multiple quarters. Qualcomm's royalty revenue fell from $7.7B in FY2016 to $4.7B in FY2018 (a 39% decline over two years) as Apple and its contract manufacturers withheld payments and Apple accelerated work on Intel modems to reduce dependence. Qualcomm's NTM P/E de-rated from ~17x in early 2017 to ~10x in mid-2018. ARM is in an earlier-stage version of the same dynamic: its largest high-ASP customer (Qualcomm) has now won a court ruling that limits ARM's ability to charge higher rates. The precedent is the mechanism, not just the risk.

key_debates.debate: Debate 2: China RISC-V displacement is structural, not contained -- and the JV structure cannot protect ARM
key_debates.bear_read: China is 19-22% of ARM total revenue (ARM_market_facts.json data_gaps_and_flags: '19-22% of ARM total'). ARM_crux.json bear assumption states 'China revenue (22% of total) comes under sustained RISC-V substitution pressure as T-Head (Alibaba) scales its XUANTIE platform, reducing ARM China revenue by 20-30% over FY2027-FY2029.' The bear case goes further than mere IoT displacement. Alibaba's T-Head XUANTIE C950 is a RISC-V CPU targeted at data-center AI inference -- the same high-ASP segment where ARM's Neoverse CSS royalties are growing fastest. Chinese government policy (Made in China 2025, the RISC-V chip fund) is specifically directing capital away from Western proprietary ISAs toward open RISC-V. DigiTimes (February 2026, cited in ARM_crux.json) documents accelerating RISC-V competition in China. ARM's China JV (Arm Technology China Co.) is structurally distinct: it is governed independently and ARM has limited ability to enforce IP or pricing discipline through the JV if the JV decides to prioritize RISC-V commercial relationships to remain politically viable. A 25% China revenue decline from the ~$1.08B FY2026 base (22% x $4.92B) removes $270M of annual revenue, which is not just a top-line cut but a near-100%-margin cut (IP licensing has zero variable cost). At a 45x exit multiple on the IP business, $270M of annual revenue is worth ~$12B of EV or ~$11/share.
key_debates.break_mechanism: T-Head XUANTIE scales to data-center inference volumes by FY2028, providing Chinese hyperscalers (Alibaba Cloud, ByteDance, Baidu) an alternative to ARM Neoverse that is both domestically controlled and exempt from Western IP fees. The JV structure breaks the protection: ARM China operates under a separate leadership (Allen Wu, Arm China CEO, who has previously had governance disputes with ARM Holdings) and cannot be compelled by ARM Holdings to resist pricing competition from RISC-V alternatives. The mechanism is visible: RISC-V at 25% unit penetration in IoT/embedded ahead of schedule (ARM_crux.json driver_3_ceiling.risc_v_floor) is the training-wheels run; data-center RISC-V is the next act. Watching for: ARM's China geographic revenue segment disclosure each quarter; T-Head public announcements of data-center RISC-V deployments; any ARM China governance change or JV restructuring.
key_debates.what_it_is_worth: China revenue ~$1.08B (22% x $4.92B FY2026). A sustained 25% erosion is $270M/year by FY2029. At 17.05x exit multiple (Daedalus's Gordon multiple) discounted at 10.5% over 4 years: $270M x 17.05 / 1.4909 = $3.09B EV reduction = ~$2.90/share. If the erosion reaches 40% (the more aggressive scenario, consistent with government-directed substitution), that is $432M/year: ~$4.93/share below base. Combined with the Qualcomm rate cap, the two mechanisms together subtract $10-14/share from Daedalus's $57.36 base.
evidence: ARM_crux.json bear.evidence[2]: 'China revenue 19-22% of total, with Alibaba T-Head building RISC-V infrastructure and Chinese government directing funding away from Western proprietary architectures. DigiTimes (February 2026) documents the accelerating RISC-V competition in China.'

ARM_crux.json driver_3_ceiling.risc_v_floor: 'RISC-V is capturing new low-ASP IoT design wins... If RISC-V accelerates, ARM's unit volume declines even as smartphone is flat.' IoT is a precursor; the same toolchain maturation enables data-center RISC-V.

ARM_crux.json key_debates[2].bear_read: 'Alibaba T-Head is building RISC-V for AI inference (XuanTie C950 RISC-V CPU for data center). Chinese government funding is specifically directed at reducing ARM dependency... ARM has limited control if the JV decides to prioritize RISC-V partnerships.'

ARM_market_facts.json industry_log_entry.dominant_theme: 'AI data-center architecture shift from x86 to ARM-based custom silicon' -- the same narrative that benefits ARM globally creates the template for RISC-V substitution in China, where T-Head is replicating the custom-silicon playbook on an open ISA.
RISC-V 25% unit penetration in embedded/IoT ahead of 2030 forecast (ARM_crux.json sources[3], Tom's Hardware, January 2026).
key_debates.historical_precedent: MIPS Technologies in the 2000s-2010s: MIPS held a dominant position in embedded/networking processors and attempted to maintain proprietary ISA licensing in markets (particularly Chinese embedded devices) where open alternatives were emerging. RISC-V's predecessor dynamic played out through ARM's own rise -- MIPS lost embedded share to ARM when ARM's open-licensing model proved more attractive than MIPS's royalty structure. ARM is now the incumbent in a parallel situation: an open-standard alternative (RISC-V) with government and hyperscaler backing is disrupting the royalty-based model in a specific geography.

key_debates.debate: Debate 3: The 143x NTM P/E collapses when the AGI CPU revenue ramp proves slower than the market's embedded assumption
key_debates.bear_read: The stock's 143x NTM P/E (ARM_headline.json justified_multiple.current_pe_ntm) and the 283% YTD rally (ARM_market_facts.json price_action.ytd_pct) are priced on the market's belief that ARM's FY2031 plan ($15B chip + $10B IP = $25B revenue) executes on schedule. The market has essentially moved the intrinsic value question from 'what is today's IP business worth' to 'what is the 2031 chip company worth.' ARM_market_facts.json market_view.what_is_priced_in: 'the market is betting that ARM hits its own five-year plan, on schedule, without supply-chain disruption or competitive share loss.' The first live test is Q1 FY2027 earnings (July 29, 2026). Management guided Q1 FY2027 revenue at $1.26B +/- $50M (ARM_market_facts.json earnings_calendar.guidance_revenue) -- below some bulls' models. The AGI CPU production revenues are not until Q4 FY2027 ($90M implied). If the Q1 print confirms anything other than a clean beat-and-raise, the momentum that carried the stock from $220 (post-May earnings) to $440 (June 18, 52-week high) collapses. The re-rating mechanism: the stock is priced for Bernstein and Mizuho's $500 targets (both raised June 2026, 13.8% above spot), and the most bullish analyst on the street has essentially no upside left to give. Any execution miss narrows the spread between current price and the best-case target, which forces de-rating even on unchanged fundamentals.
key_debates.break_mechanism: AGI CPU supply unlocks slower than the market's embedded timeline (Q4 FY2027 $90M is the first revenue, but the street is pricing a J-curve acceleration into FY2028). ARM disclosed supply is locked for only $1B of the $20B+ in stated demand (ARM_market_facts.json news_narrative) -- meaning $19B+ of demand cannot be monetized near-term. TSMC CoWoS advanced packaging is the bottleneck; the same supply constraint that throttled NVIDIA's Blackwell ramp applies to ARM's AGI CPU. If Q2 or Q3 FY2027 guidance does not show the supply ramp accelerating, the street's FY2028 AGI CPU revenue models ($3-5B) must be cut, and the 143x NTM P/E loses the forward earnings power that justifies it. Additionally: every quarter the AGI CPU is supply-constrained is a quarter where ARM's licensees (Qualcomm, MediaTek) continue selling their own ARM-based designs -- and when ARM's chip eventually ships, it competes with those licensees, potentially souring the relationship that underlies the royalty business. Morgan Stanley cited this explicitly in its April 2026 downgrade (ARM_market_facts.json analyst_tape.post_earnings_analyst_actions[0]).
key_debates.what_it_is_worth: From a multiple compression standpoint, the bear case is a compression from 143x to 40x NTM non-GAAP P/E. At 40x on the $3.07 NTM EPS consensus (ARM_market_facts.json estimate_artifact_check.eps_ntm_bundle), the stock is worth $122.80 -- a 72% decline from $439. The 40x target is still a significant premium to IP-licensor mature peers (Qualcomm 15x, Dolby 20x, InterDigital 13x -- ARM_valuation.json cross_check.reasoning) but prices in ARM's structural growth advantage without pricing the AGI CPU moonshot. At 20x (the peer IP-licensor premium for a company in a genuine structural growth position), the relative floor is $61.40 -- essentially our intrinsic base of $57.36, confirming the range is coherent. The de-rating from 143x to 40x -- without any fundamental deterioration -- is worth $316.66 per share in price destruction. This is the largest single component of the bear case and has no dependency on the Qualcomm or China debates firing.
evidence: ARM_market_facts.json price_action.ytd_pct: +283% YTD return; the stock bottomed at $100 52-week low and trades at 4.4x that level; the run is momentum-driven by AGI CPU narrative, not by a 283% improvement in trailing earnings.

ARM_market_facts.json analyst_tape.target_high: $500 (Mizuho, Bernstein); ARM at $439.46 is already 87.9% of the way to the highest sell-side target on the street, leaving 13.8% upside to the most optimistic case. Risk/reward is asymmetric.

ARM_market_facts.json earnings_calendar.notes: 'A miss on revenue vs the $1.26B midpoint, or any downward revision to the AGI CPU ramp timeline, would pressure the stock materially given the current pricing.'

ARM_market_facts.json market_view.what_is_priced_in: the stock requires $15-25B in long-run annual revenue at sustained high margins to justify its current EV of ~$465B -- this is exactly management's FY2031 five-year target, with zero margin for execution error.

ARM_market_facts.json news_narrative: 'supply locked for only $1B of the $20B+ in stated demand' -- the supply gap is observable, not theoretical; the J-curve acceleration the street has modeled is not yet visible in contract terms.

ARM_headline.json four_numbers.price_today.multiple: 221.6x EV/NTM non-GAAP NOPAT (estimated), versus the justified 28.0x in the base case -- the gap is 8x the justified multiple; de-rating to 40x NTM P/E is not bearish, it is merely not euphoric.
key_debates.historical_precedent: NVIDIA's multiple compression in 2022: NVIDIA traded at 70-90x NTM P/E in late 2021 on the expectation that gaming and metaverse would sustain hyper-growth. When the gaming cycle turned and the metaverse narrative collapsed in Q1 2022, NVIDIA de-rated from 80x to 20x NTM P/E over 12 months -- a 75% multiple compression -- even as NVIDIA's underlying GPU architecture and data-center long-term thesis remained intact. The stock fell from $330 to $110 despite no change in the structural AI compute thesis that would later drive it to $900. ARM today is in a similar position: the structural thesis (ARM CPU in AI data centers) is arguably real, but at 143x NTM P/E, any delay in the specific near-term catalyst (AGI CPU revenue ramp) triggers NVIDIA-2022-style de-rating. The difference is NVIDIA's decline was a one-year event; ARM's multiple is even further from peers.

structural_forensic_check.cash_conversion: FY2026 FCF of $0.949B vs non-GAAP NI of $1.883B: cash conversion ratio 50.4%. This is poor for a pure IP licensor -- the gap is explained by elevated capex ($0.575B, up from $0.143B in FY2024) for AGI CPU chip infrastructure and SBC (~$0.50B estimated). ARM_valuation.json earnings_bridge documents this. The concern: as ARM pivots into chip design, capex will not 'normalize down' quickly. The bull case models capex declining as AGI CPU is a fabless model (no fab); but test/packaging/design costs are real. The structural FCF conversion in the bear case is 45-50%, not the 80%+ conversion of a pure IP business.
structural_forensic_check.receivables_vs_revenue: No segment-level receivables disclosed in the available bundle (ARM_market_facts.json data_gaps_and_flags notes EDGAR not fetched). Flag: cannot verify receivables-to-revenue ratio. This is a data gap, not a finding.
structural_forensic_check.sbc_as_economic_cost: SBC estimated at ~$0.50B in FY2026 (ARM_valuation.json earnings_bridge.sbc_estimated_b), implying SBC / non-GAAP NI = 26.6%. At the current market cap of ~$467B, annual SBC represents ~0.1% annual dilution -- not existential but real. Non-GAAP NI of $1.883B overstates owner earnings by $0.50B relative to the cash statement. The market's 143x NTM P/E is on non-GAAP; on a cash-earnings basis at 15% FCF margin (FY2026), the implied cash earnings multiple exceeds 400x. This is the real entry price.
structural_forensic_check.margin_sustainability: The base case models NOPAT margin rising from 38% (FY2026) to 48% (FY2030). The bear challenge: as AGI CPU scales, R&D and supply chain costs rise faster than IP royalty margin. ARM management guides ~38% non-GAAP net margin at $25B revenue in FY2031 (ARM_crux.json crux_argued.bear.evidence implicitly). If the chip business reaches $15B at chip-company margins (~40-45% gross, ~20-25% operating), blending with the IP business at 97.5% gross margin produces a blended structure significantly below the 48% NOPAT base. In the bear case, NOPAT margin in FY2030 is 43-45%, not 48%, reducing FY2030 NOPAT by approximately $225-$450M. At 17.05x exit multiple, that is a $3.8-7.7B EV reduction, or $3.60-7.20/share.
structural_forensic_check.buyback_quality: No buyback or dividend program in the data (ARM_market_facts.json positioning_notes). SoftBank holds the majority economic interest; ARM's capital return posture is aligned with the parent's cash needs, not minority shareholders. In the bear case, SoftBank's leverage position (it has historically used ARM equity as collateral) creates overhang risk if ARM's stock declines materially -- SoftBank may be forced to sell ARM shares to meet margin calls, amplifying any fundamental-driven decline.

scenario_spec.vs_daedalus_base: Bear revenue FY2030 $6.5B vs Daedalus base $9.3B: the gap is driven by (1) Qualcomm ALA precedent limiting CSS migration to TLA-only licensees, slowing royalty revenue to ~10-12% CAGR vs 17.2% base; (2) China revenue erosion of 25% ($270M/yr by FY2029) from T-Head RISC-V in data-center AI inference; (3) NOPAT margin 45% vs 48% base due to AGI CPU R&D drag and China revenue mix loss. AGI CPU option reduced to $0.3B (vs $2.0B base) reflecting supply delay + licensee conflict. Interim FCF PV $4.45B vs $5.73B base. Net: $38.11 bear intrinsic vs $57.36 base -- a $19.25/share discount to Daedalus's base.
scenario_spec.bear_interim_fcf_derivation: Year 1 FY2027: NOPAT $2.0B (vs $2.39B base), reinvest 27%, FCF $1.46B, PV at 10.5% $1.32B. Year 2 FY2028: NOPAT $2.35B, reinvest 22%, FCF $1.83B, PV $1.50B. Year 3 FY2029: NOPAT $2.65B, reinvest 17%, FCF $2.20B, PV $1.63B. Sum $4.45B. Lower than Daedalus bear interim PV of $5.24B due to harder revenue assumption in FY2027-FY2028 as Qualcomm precedent suppresses CSS renegotiations.

multiple_compression_argument.rationale: The bear target of 40x NTM non-GAAP P/E is not a distressed multiple: it is 2.7x Qualcomm's through-cycle 15x P/E, 2x mature IP licensor comps (InterDigital 13x, Dolby 20x), and roughly equal to high-growth software companies (Synopsys/Cadence at 40-50x NTM P/E). It prices ARM as a premium high-growth IP licensor without pricing the AGI CPU chip business optionality. The de-rating mechanism is named: (1) AGI CPU first revenue appears in Q4 FY2027 at $90M -- if this is the only data point the market gets before FY2028, every quarter between now and then is a 'proof of plan' void; (2) the Qualcomm appeal outcome (a binary event likely in late 2026 or 2027) removes the CSS rate migration upside permanently if ARM loses; (3) the two Street-high targets at $500 (Bernstein, Mizuho) leave only 13.8% upside to the most optimistic analyst on record -- the asymmetry is already unfavorable without any fundamental deterioration. Historical parallel: Arm traded at 90x NTM P/E in late 2023 at IPO; it re-rated to 60-70x in H1 2024 on the AI build-out thesis, then accelerated to 143x in 2025-2026 on AGI CPU demand. A retracement to 40x NTM P/E would still be well above ARM's pre-AI IPO valuation context and is the conservative bear floor, not a crash scenario. At 20x (the IP-licensor peer average if ARM fails to demonstrate chip business scale), the implied price is $61.40 -- essentially intrinsic value, a 86% decline. The 40x target is the 'things go slightly worse than the base but not catastrophically' scenario: 72% price decline while the business remains healthy.
de_rating_precedents: NVIDIA 2022: 80x to 20x NTM P/E compression over 12 months on narrative shift from metaverse to AI capex pause; underlying data-center thesis unchanged. Stock -75% peak to trough.
Qualcomm 2017-2019: royalty rate dispute with Apple drove 40% earnings cut and NTM P/E de-rated from 17x to 10x. Stock -40% over 2 years.

ARM Holdings pre-IPO analog: MIPS Technologies attempted IP royalty expansion and was ultimately acquired for $100M in 2013 after losing smartphone share to ARM. ARM today is in ARM's role; RISC-V is in ARM's role. History rhymes on the licensee-vs-ISA dynamic.
